name: 金属的物理与化学性质
subject: chemistry
curriculum_points: 常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。
能独立或与他人合作开展化学实验，收集证据；能基于事实，分析证据与假设的关系，形成结论。

deep_snippets.text: or of the transition metals with higher oxidation states is exemplified by the reaction of the metal tetrahalides with water. Like covalent silicon tetrachloride, both the titanium and vanadium tetrahalides react with water to give solutions containing the corresponding hydrohalic acids and the metal oxides: SiCl4(l) + 2H2 O(l) ⟶SiO2(s) + 4HCl(aq) TiCl4(l) + 2H2 O(l) ⟶TiO2(s) + 4HCl(aq) Oxides As with the halides, the nature of bonding in oxides of the transition elements is determined by the oxidation state of the metal. Oxides with low oxidation states tend to be more ionic, whereas those with higher oxidation states are more covalent. These variations in bonding are because the electronegativities of the elements are not fixed values. The electronegativity of an element increases with increasing oxidation state. Transition metals in low oxidation states have lower electronegativity values than oxygen; therefore, these metal oxides are ionic. Transition metals in very high oxidation states have electronegativity values close to that of oxygen, which leads to these oxides being covalent. The oxides of the first transition series can be prepared by heating the metals in air. These oxides are Sc2O3, TiO2, V2O5, Cr2O3, Mn3O4, Fe3O4, Co3O4, NiO, and CuO. Alternatively, these oxides and other oxides (with the metals in different oxidation states) can be produced by heating the corresponding hydroxides, carbonates, or oxalates in an inert atmosphere. Iron(II) oxide can be prepared by heating iron(II) oxalate, and cobalt(II) oxide is produced by heating cobalt(II) hydroxide: FeC2

Figure 18.28 Before the fleet’s retirement in 2011, liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen were used in the three main engines of a space shuttle. Two compartments in the large tank held these liquids until the shuttle was launched. (credit: “reynermedia”/Flickr) An uncombined hydrogen atom consists of a nucleus and one valence electron in the 1s orbital. The n = 1 valence shell has a capacity for two electrons, and hydrogen can rightfully occupy two locations in the periodic table. It is possible to consider hydrogen a group 1 element because hydrogen can lose an electron to form the cation, H+. It is also possible to consider hydrogen to be a group 17 element because it needs only one electron to fill its valence orbital to form a hydride ion, H−, or it can share an electron to form a single, covalent bond. In reality, hydrogen is a unique element that almost deserves its own location in the periodic table. Reactions with Elements When heated, hydrogen reacts with the metals of group 1 and with Ca, Sr, and Ba (the more active metals in group 2). The compounds formed are crystalline, ionic hydrides that contain the hydride anion, H−, a strong reducing agent and a strong base, which reacts vigorously with water and other acids to form hydrogen gas. The reactions of hydrogen with nonmetals generally produce acidic hydrogen compounds with hydrogen in the 1+ oxidation state. The reactions become more exothermic and vigorous as the electronegativity of the nonmetal increases. Hydrogen reacts with nitrogen and sulfur only when heated, but it reacts explosively with fluorine (forming

Chapter 18 | Representative Metals, Metalloids, and Nonmetals This OpenStax book is available for free at http://cnx.org/content/col11760/1.9 > **[Figure 715, Page 1012]** A large illustration or diagram (650×509px) related to: Alkali, metal, hydrogen, carbonates, form, saturating. This image appears on page 1012 of the textbook. ![Figure 715 - A large illustration or diagram (650×509px) related to: Alka](OpenStax_Chemistry_images/img_0715_c8551e7f.png) Figure 18.31 The reaction of calcium carbonate with hydrochloric acid is shown. (credit: Mark Ott) Other applications of carbonates include glass making—where carbonate ions serve as a source of oxide ions—and synthesis of oxides. Hydrogen carbonates are amphoteric because they act as both weak acids and weak bases. Hydrogen carbonate ions act as acids and react with solutions of soluble hydroxides to form a carbonate and water: KHCO3(aq) + KOH(aq) ⟶K2 CO3(aq) + H2 O(l) With acids, hydrogen carbonates form a salt, carbon dioxide, and water. Baking soda (bicarbonate of soda or sodium bicarbonate) is sodium hydrogen carbonate. Baking powder contains baking soda and a solid acid such as potassium hydrogen tartrate (cream of tartar), KHC4H4O6. As long as the powder is dry, no reaction occurs; immediately after the addition of water, the acid reacts with the hydrogen carbonate ions to form carbon dioxide:

supplements.curriculum_md_raw:
# 金属的物理与化学性质

## 基本信息
- **学科**: 化学
- **学段**: middle
- **年级**: 9
- **章节/领域**: 物质的性质与应用
- **内容来源**: TeachAny 课标知识点卫星文件

## 一、课标原文

### 1.1 内容要求
> 常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。
> 能独立或与他人合作开展化学实验，收集证据；能基于事实，分析证据与假设的关系，形成结论。

## 二、教学目标

- 理解「金属的物理与化学性质」的核心概念、方法与应用场景。
- 能结合材料、问题或真实情境解释「金属的物理与化学性质」相关现象。
- 能用规范的学科语言表达思路、依据和结论。

## 三、教学重难点

- 教学重点：建立对「金属的物理与化学性质」核心概念和基本方法的准确理解。
- 教学难点：把知识迁移到新的问题、材料或真实情境中。

## 五、教学建议

### 5.1 教学方法
- 从实验现象、微观粒子和符号表达三个层面讲解「金属的物理与化学性质」，让学生把现象与本质对应起来。
- 重视实验安全、证据记录、化学用语规范和条件限制，避免只背结论。
- 围绕课标要点“常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。”，安排观察、解释、书写表达和应用判断任务。

### 5.2 学习活动设计
1. 从实验现象、微观粒子和符号表达三个层面讲解「金属的物理与化学性质」，让学生把现象与本质对应起来。
2. 重视实验安全、证据记录、化学用语规范和条件限制，避免只背结论。
3. 围绕课标要点“常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。”，安排观察、解释、书写表达和应用判断任务。

## 七、课件制作参考

- 从实验现象、微观粒子和符号表达三个层面讲解「金属的物理与化学性质」，让学生把现象与本质对应起来。
- 重视实验安全、证据记录、化学用语规范和条件限制，避免只背结论。
- 围绕课标要点“常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。”，安排观察、解释、书写表达和应用判断任务。

## 八、相关知识扩展

- 联系材料、环境、医药或食品中的案例，讨论「金属的物理与化学性质」的实际价值。
- 补充安全实验、绿色化学或定量分析任务，提升证据意识。
supplements.textbook_summary:
## 教材化讲解参考（由课标要点生成）

### 核心概念
金属的物理与化学性质是化学学习中的重要知识点。教学时应围绕课标要点“常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。”展开，先明确概念边界，再通过例子、图示、实验、语篇或案例帮助学生建立可迁移的理解。

### 学习路径
1. 情境导入：从实验现象、微观粒子和符号表达三个层面讲解「金属的物理与化学性质」，让学生把现象与本质对应起来。
2. 探究建构：重视实验安全、证据记录、化学用语规范和条件限制，避免只背结论。
3. 应用迁移：围绕课标要点“常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。”，安排观察、解释、书写表达和应用判断任务。

### 例题设计方向
可设计一道“识别核心概念”的基础题，一道“解释现象或文本材料”的理解题，一道“迁移到真实情境”的应用题，并要求学生说明依据。

deep_worked_example.solution_outline: 先描述现象或实验条件，再指出关键概念和证据，最后给出解释或结论。

errors.description: 只记方程式不说明反应条件与类型
忽视实验操作与安全意识

exercises.stem: 依据课标「金属的物理与化学性质」：常见金属的物理性质和化学性质。 请简要阐述并举例。
exercises.answer: 应答覆盖课标要点，体现掌握与应用。

exercises.stem: 比较说明：能独立或与他人合作开展化学实验，收集证据；能基于事实，分析证据与假设的关系，形成结论。
exercises.answer: 需写出关键概念、过程或方法，避免只背结论。

real_world: 从厨房、清洁用品、材料变化或环境问题中寻找与「金属的物理与化学性质」相关的化学现象。
